But when the surface<|14.56|> <|14.56|> is scratched, Hananiah passes none of those tests because of our next point, point two.<|23.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> False prophets are date-specific.<|5.56|> <|5.56|> It is often said in scholarly circles that prophets are not fortune-tellers.<|12.24|> <|12.24|> My friend, Minister Candice Yonina Simpson, says that the prophets are so in touch with the present<|19.76|> <|19.76|> that they appear to predict the future.<|23.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But this is different from fortune telling.<|2.86|> <|3.68|> Hananiah is a fortune teller here.<|6.32|> <|7.48|> Verse 3.<|8.02|> <|9.22|> Hananiah puts a date on Judah's good fortune returning.<|13.74|> <|15.14|> Quote, in another two years, God will bring back to this place all the vessels of the house of the Lord that the king of Babylon took from it.<|24.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and all the exiles of Judah who came to Babylon, I will bring back to this place.<|5.96|> <|7.52|> Imagine being in Hananiah's audience and hearing this for the first time.<|12.10|> <|13.12|> Your family has been ripped apart due to Babylonian conquest and captivity,<|17.80|> <|17.80|> and he is planting in your heart a glimmer of hope about them returning home.<|23.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You will hold on to any good news you can get.<|4.30|> <|5.30|> Instead of openly castigating Hananiah's date-specific two years prophecy,<|11.78|> <|12.86|> the prophet Jeremiah contrasts it.<|17.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In verse 6, he says this,<|2.64|> <|3.76|> Amen.<|3.98|> <|5.08|> May the Lord fulfill the words that you have prophesied<|7.70|> <|7.70|> and bring back to this place from Babylon<|10.00|> <|10.00|> the vessels of the house of the Lord and all the exiles.<|14.00|> <|16.34|> Jeremiah mirrors Hananiah's prophecy, but drops the date from it.<|21.82|> <|23.26|> This hits particularly close to home, doesn't it?<|26.98|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The easy way to navigate these times in which we live is to be date-driven and date-specific.<|8.74|> <|9.90|> The easy thing to say is everything will return to normal on Monday, October 5th at 10 a.m. Mountain Time.<|19.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The easy thing to say is the coronavirus will go away without a vaccine.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> It'll just go away. We've heard that in the news.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> The easy route is the route of the false prophet,<|18.00|> <|18.00|> preying on a population looking for any semblance of normalcy,<|23.00|> <|23.00|> of a return to things as they were before the pandemic.<|28.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The easy thing is to dole out promises based neither in science, data, nor facts.<|10.20|> <|10.20|> Just the empty voicing of words that mean nothing.<|14.24|> <|14.24|> "Oh yes," Hananiah seems to say, "they'll be okay with any old date.<|20.68|> <|20.68|> Yeah, two years.<|22.80|> <|22.80|> That sounds right to say."<|24.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This kind of reckless leadership must frustrate those prophets who are themselves driven by science, facts, and data.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> This brings us to our final point in how to spot a false prophet.<|19.00|> <|19.00|> False prophets are optimistic, while true prophets are hopeful.<|28.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Notice that Hananiah's pronouncements are brimming with optimism.<|6.88|> <|7.60|> The things his audience wants to hear instead of those things that will be most helpful to them.<|14.34|> <|15.80|> In a theological context, optimism says things will be okay.<|22.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> while hope says I will be okay.<|4.46|> <|4.46|> Optimism says things will be okay.<|7.14|> <|7.64|> Hope says I will be okay.<|9.70|> <|11.02|> Optimism is tied to circumstances,<|14.18|> <|14.52|> while hope is tied to my and your inner landscape of trust in God's love and faithfulness.<|23.32|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Hananiah's optimism gives his audience a false hope, a devastation that will push them even<|8.54|> <|8.54|> deeper into their reality of exile.<|13.30|> <|13.30|> In times of intense feelings, intense external circumstances, intense partisanship, and<|19.96|> <|19.96|> intense civic unrest, the last thing one needs to hear is a promise meant to temporarily<|26.62|> <|26.62|> assuage despair.<|28.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.18|> Optimism provides momentary euphoria,<|3.92|> <|4.78|> but it is no match to the long-term joy<|8.38|> <|8.38|> that sustained, deeply rooted hope brings.<|12.22|> <|14.00|> And this is where this portion of Scripture leaves us today,<|17.94|> <|18.50|> with hope.<|19.98|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> That when prophets like Jeremiah, prophets interested in substance over sneakiness, data over dates, and hope over optimism, when those prophets speak peace over our lives and society, they are speaking from deep wells of wisdom.<|18.68|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They can be trusted.<|2.18|> <|4.16|> And those prophets are on our streets, in our classrooms, at home with us,<|12.46|> <|12.96|> creating art and joy and hope and love at every turn.<|17.36|> <|18.70|> And we thank God for them.<|20.90|> <|22.58|> Oh, we thank God.<|23.94|> <|25.28|> Amen.<|26.06|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.14|><|endoftext|>
